A lawsuit brought by a shareholder on behalf of the corporation. It is used when the corporation itself has been harmed and managers refuse to sue.
The right of shareholders to vote on major corporate matters, such as electing directors or approving major changes. Voting power usually depends on the number and type of shares owned.
The group elected by shareholders to oversee a corporation's major decisions and management. The board hires officers, sets direction, and protects shareholder interests.

Taxation of corporate income once at the corporate level and again when profits are distributed to shareholders as dividends. It is a major tax disadvantage of regular C corporations.
Tax treatment where business profits pass to owners and are taxed on their personal returns. It helps avoid entity-level income tax for partnerships, many LLCs, and S corporations.
Land and things permanently attached to land, such as buildings. Real property rules matter for leases, sales, mortgages, zoning, and business locations.
Property that is not land or permanently attached to land. It includes equipment, inventory, vehicles, furniture, and many business assets.
Physical property that can be touched. Examples include land, machines, inventory, computers, and office furniture.
Property rights that do not have a physical form. Examples include patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade secrets, and contract rights.
Personal property that becomes treated as real property because it is attached to land or a building. Businesses should clarify fixtures in leases and property sales.
Legal ownership of property. Title matters because it determines who can sell, transfer, or claim rights in the property.
Physical control or holding of property. Possession is not always the same as ownership, but it can create legal rights and duties.
A temporary transfer of possession of personal property without transfer of ownership. Examples include storage, repairs, rentals, and valet parking.
The owner or holder who gives personal property to someone else temporarily. The bailor keeps ownership while the bailee has possession.
The party who temporarily receives and controls someone else's personal property. The bailee must take proper care of the property and return it as agreed.
The level of care a bailee must use for property in their possession. The required care depends on who benefits from the bailment and the circumstances.
The broadest ownership interest in real property. A fee simple owner generally has the right to use, sell, lease, or transfer the property, subject to law.
A right to use someone else's land for a specific purpose. Common examples include driveways, utility lines, and access roads.
Permission to use property for a limited purpose without creating an ownership interest. A license is usually easier to revoke than an easement or lease.
A tenant's temporary right to possess and use real property under a lease. Businesses often use leaseholds for offices, stores, warehouses, and equipment yards.
The property owner who leases real estate to a tenant. The landlord's rights and duties come from the lease and property law.
The party who rents and uses property under a lease. Tenants must usually pay rent, follow lease terms, and avoid damaging the property.
A lease for business property rather than residential living. Commercial leases often involve negotiated terms for rent, repairs, insurance, taxes, assignment, and use restrictions.
A lease where the original tenant rents part or all of the leased property to another tenant. The original tenant usually remains responsible to the landlord unless released.
A government-granted right to exclude others from making, using, or selling an invention for a limited time. Patents can protect valuable technology and create competitive advantage.
A patent that protects how an invention works or is used. It commonly covers machines, processes, chemicals, software-related inventions, and useful improvements.
A patent that protects the ornamental design or appearance of a useful product. It does not protect how the product works.
The legal ability of an invention to qualify for patent protection. An invention usually must be useful, novel, nonobvious, and proper subject matter.
A patent requirement that the invention must be new. If the same invention was already publicly known, used, sold, or published, it may not be patentable.
A patent requirement that the invention must not be an obvious improvement to someone skilled in the field. It prevents patents on small, routine changes.
Making, using, selling, or importing a patented invention without permission. Businesses must check patent rights before launching products or processes.
Legal protection for original creative expression, such as writing, music, software code, videos, and artwork. Copyright protects expression, not general ideas.
A creative work made independently with at least a small amount of creativity. It is the basic kind of work that can receive copyright protection.
The requirement that a copyrighted work be captured in a stable form, such as writing, recording, saving, or filming. Copyright does not protect a purely spoken or temporary idea unless fixed.
A work created by an employee within the job scope or certain commissioned works under written agreement. The employer or hiring party may be treated as the copyright owner.
A copyright defense allowing limited use of protected work for purposes like criticism, teaching, research, news, or commentary. Courts weigh factors such as purpose, amount used, and market effect.
Using protected expression without permission or a valid defense. Businesses face risk when copying images, music, software, text, or video content.
A word, symbol, name, logo, or design that identifies the source of goods. Trademarks help customers know who made or sells a product.
A trademark used to identify services instead of goods. Examples include brand names or logos for consulting, banking, restaurants, or software services.

The main test for trademark infringement, asking whether consumers are likely to be confused about the source of goods or services. Businesses should avoid names and logos that are too similar to existing brands in related markets.
Harm to a famous mark's distinctiveness or reputation, even without consumer confusion. It protects very well-known brands from blurring or tarnishment.
The overall look and feel of a product or business that identifies its source. It can include packaging, layout, design, colors, or store appearance if distinctive.
Valuable business information kept secret that gives a competitive advantage. Examples include formulas, customer lists, processes, pricing methods, and confidential strategies.
Steps a business must take to keep trade secret protection, such as NDAs, access limits, passwords, and employee policies. Information loses trade secret status if the business does not protect it.
Wrongfully acquiring, using, or disclosing a trade secret. It often involves theft, broken confidentiality promises, or improper access.
A contract requiring someone to keep certain information confidential. Businesses use NDAs when sharing sensitive information with employees, contractors, investors, or partners.

An employment relationship where either the employer or employee can end the job at any time for almost any lawful reason. Employers still cannot fire workers for illegal reasons such as discrimination or retaliation.
An unlawful firing that violates a contract, statute, or public policy. Businesses reduce this risk by documenting legitimate reasons and applying policies consistently.
A legal test used to decide whether a worker is truly an independent contractor or should be treated as an employee. Misclassification can create tax, wage, benefit, and liability problems.
Laws that regulate pay, minimum wage, overtime, recordkeeping, and child labor. Employers must track hours and classify workers correctly to avoid penalties.
Extra pay owed to covered employees who work more than the legal threshold, usually over 40 hours in a workweek under federal law. Overtime Pay Rate = Regular Hourly Rate × 1.5.
A classification that determines whether an employee is covered by overtime rules. Nonexempt employees must receive overtime pay, while exempt employees usually do not if they meet legal tests.
An employer's duty to provide a reasonably safe place to work. Safety failures can lead to injuries, OSHA penalties, lawsuits, and productivity losses.
Federal workplace safety rules enforced by the Occupational Safety and Health Administration. Businesses must follow relevant standards, train workers, and keep required safety records.

Punishing an employee for legally protected activity, such as reporting discrimination, safety issues, or wage violations. Retaliation claims can succeed even when the original complaint is not proven.
Intentional discrimination where an employer treats someone worse because of a protected characteristic. It can appear in hiring, firing, pay, promotion, discipline, or scheduling.
A neutral workplace rule that disproportionately harms a protected group and is not job-related or necessary. Employers must check whether policies create unfair results even without bad intent.
A legally protected trait such as race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, disability, or genetic information. Employment decisions cannot be based on protected characteristics.
Harassment that is severe or repeated enough to make the workplace abusive or intimidating. Employers can be liable if they fail to prevent or correct it.
A workplace adjustment that helps an employee with a disability or religious need perform the job or follow their beliefs. Employers must provide it unless it creates undue hardship.
Harassment where a job benefit or punishment is tied to submitting to unwelcome sexual conduct. It creates serious employer liability risk, especially when supervisors are involved.
An agreement limiting where or for whom an employee can work after leaving a job. Enforceability depends on current law and whether the restriction is reasonable and lawful.
Confidential business information employees learn through their work that gives the employer an advantage. Employers protect it with access controls, policies, NDAs, and exit procedures.
A country's power to govern itself and make its own laws. In international business, sovereignty affects regulation, courts, enforcement, and government control over trade.
A contract clause or legal rule deciding which jurisdiction's law applies to a dispute. It helps businesses reduce uncertainty in cross-border or multi-state deals.
A contract clause deciding where disputes will be heard, such as a specific court or country. It helps businesses avoid fighting over location after a dispute begins.

A contract clause that excuses or delays performance when extraordinary events beyond the parties' control occur. Examples may include natural disasters, war, strikes, or government actions, depending on the wording.

A private dispute resolution process where neutral arbitrators decide cross-border business disputes. Companies often prefer it because awards may be easier to enforce internationally than court judgments.
Law that prohibits offering, paying, requesting, or receiving improper payments to influence decisions. Businesses need compliance systems because bribery risks increase in global markets.
A U.S. law that prohibits bribing foreign officials to get or keep business and requires accurate books and internal controls. It applies to many U.S. companies and people operating abroad.
Rules that restrict sending certain goods, technology, software, or information to other countries or people. Businesses must screen exports because violations can lead to major penalties.
Government restrictions on doing business with certain countries, organizations, industries, or people. Businesses must check customers, suppliers, and transactions against sanctions lists.
The study or comparison of different legal systems. For business, it helps managers understand how contracts, courts, liability, and regulation differ across countries.
A legal system where court decisions and precedent play a major role along with statutes. The United States and United Kingdom are common law examples.
A legal system based mainly on written codes and statutes rather than judge-made precedent. Many countries in Europe, Latin America, and Asia use civil law systems.
